notes.note: Tyre was the great Phoenician trading city, and Carthage the still greater colony its merchants founded on the North African coast in 814 BC. Calling New Bedford the Tyre of this Carthage makes Nantucket the daughter-port that outgrew its parent, casting the whaling towns as a new commercial empire risen from the sea.

The Thirty Years' War (1618-1648) was a long, ruinous series of European conflicts that left soldiers scarred and maimed for life. Ishmael compares Queequeg's battered, marked face to a veteran of that war, reading his tattoos and scars as the record of hard campaigning before he learns they are something stranger.

Thomas Becket, archbishop of Canterbury, was cut down by the king's knights inside Canterbury Cathedral on December 29, 1170, and at once venerated as a martyr; the spot where he fell became England's most visited pilgrim shrine. The grim image measures how worn and worshipped the cabin threshold has become under generations of feet.

The Thirty-Nine Articles are the formal doctrinal statement of the Church of England, the official summary of its Protestant beliefs. Ishmael invokes them as a byword for rigid, institutional religion, the opposite of the wordless, practical faith he is coming to respect in Queequeg.

The Quakers, or Society of Friends, are a Protestant movement that rejects formal clergy and outward ceremony for the inward light of conscience, and plain speech using thee and thou. Quakers settled Nantucket and dominated its whaling, which is why the Pequod's owners speak in Quaker plain-style even while driving hard bargains.

Socrates was the Athenian philosopher of the fifth century BC who taught by relentless questioning and claimed that real wisdom begins in knowing how little one knows. Ishmael credits Queequeg with that same calm, unschooled depth, ranking the supposed savage with the founder of Western philosophy.

John Bunyan, the tinker-preacher who wrote The Pilgrim's Progress, spent twelve years in an English jail for holding worship without a license, and composed much of his great allegory there. Melville names him to argue that God hands literary genius to the poor and imprisoned as readily as to the comfortable.

notes.anchor: stumped and paupered arm of old Cervantes
notes.note: Miguel de Cervantes, author of Don Quixote, was crippled in the left hand by a wound at the sea-battle of Lepanto in 1571, then captured by Barbary corsairs and held five years before returning to Spain to live in poverty. Melville cites his maimed arm as proof that greatness often comes from the broken and the poor.

Andrew Jackson was born in frontier poverty, orphaned young, and rose through fame as a backwoods military hero to become the seventh president in 1828, the great American image of the self-made man. Melville lifts him up as God's proof that supreme greatness can be flung up from the lowest and poorest ranks.

Carl Linnaeus was the Swedish naturalist who built the modern system for naming and ranking living things by genus and species. His ruling that whales are mammals, not fish, on the grounds of warm blood, lungs, and milk, is exactly the learned authority Ishmael cheerfully overrules in favor of the plain whaleman's view that a whale is a spouting fish.

The seven Imperial Electors were the German princes who, under the Golden Bull of 1356, held the sole right to elect the Holy Roman Emperor and dined with him at his coronation feast. Melville reaches for that stiff, ceremonious banquet to picture the frozen formality of Ahab presiding in silence over his three mates at the cabin table.
